content:
I’m a postdoctoral researcher with Robin Ryder in CEREMADE at Université Paris Dauphine working on problems in computational statistics and phylogenetic inference.
Before coming to Dauphine, I was a postdoc at the University of Oxford’s Department of Statistics (2016–2019) and Big Data Institute (2019–2020), and a stipendiary lecturer at St Peter’s College (2019–2020).
For my DPhil, I worked with Geoff Nicholls on a novel method to infer phylogenetic trees when evolutionary traits may transfer laterally between species. The code is on GitHub.
My email address is here.
 Download my resumé. --
summary:

Before coming to Dauphine, I was a postdoc at the University of Oxford’s Department of Statistics (2016–2019) and Big Data Institute (2019–2020), and a stipendiary lecturer at St Peter’s College (2019–2020).

content:
I consulted a number of sources (1, 2, 3 and 4, amongst others) for how to create a GitHub Action to automatically build this website using a deploy key on GitHub. The complete .yaml file is below.
As both my source and host repos are private, the action failed because it was unable to check out the host repo — creating a personal access token on GitHub and adding it as a secret to the source repo with a reference the action .yaml file solved this issue.
- uses: actions/checkout@v2 with: submodules: true fetch-depth: 0 token: ${{ secrets.PAT_TOKEN }}  Another error was that the wowchemy academic theme was not compatible with the version of Hugo. In that case, we need to specify the version of Go.
- name: Setup Go uses: actions/setup-go@v2 with: go-version: '1.15'  The dropdown menu on mobile browsers did not work when I built the website on my Macbook and manually pushed it to the host repo, but it now works when built through the GitHub action, despite using the same versions of Hugo and Go.
summary: I consulted a number of sources (1, 2, 3 and 4, amongst others) for how to create a GitHub Action to automatically build this website using a deploy key on GitHub.
title: Github Actions on private repos

content:
Install and run BEAST 2 From your home directory on the cluster, download a tarball of BEAST 2 (v2.6.3 was the latest at the time of writing) and extract the files.
wget https://github.com/CompEvol/beast2/releases/download/v2.6.3/BEAST.v2.6.3.Linux.tgz tar -xf BEAST.v2.6.3.Linux.tgz  This will create a folder beast/ in the current directory.
Next, install all of the add-on packages.
for BEAST_PACKAGE in $(java -cp beast/lib/beast.jar beast.util.PackageManager -list | tail -n +5 | cut -d" " -f1); do java -cp beast/lib/beast.jar beast.util.PackageManager -add $BEAST_PACKAGE done  We can then run BEAST 2 on an XML file, say example.xml by submitting the command
java -Djava.library.path=beast/lib/ -jar beast/lib/launcher.jar example.xml  to the queue. Instructions for submitting jobs to the SLURM queue are here.
Compute posterior distributions on topologies I use treeloganalyser in BEAST for this. First download and untar the latest release.
wget https://github.com/beast-dev/beast-mcmc/releases/download/v1.10.4/BEASTv1.10.4.tgz tar -xf BEASTv1.10.4.tgz  Then create a file called treeloganalyser in BEASTv1.10.4/bin/ containing the script on this page.
Assuming we are in the same directory as BEASTv1.10.4 and the file output.trees from a BEAST analyses, we can run treeloganalyser with
bash BEASTv1.10.4/bin/treeloganalyser output.trees  to produce a posterior summary on trees and clades.
If we want to obtain the actual posterior support figures then we first export the treeloganalyser output then use a regular expression to get the figures we want.
bash BEASTv1.10.4/bin/treeloganalyser -export tla-report.log output.trees sed -n 's/^.*\[\&W \(.*\) \].*$/\1/p' tla-report.log > tla-support.txt  Or at least we can using the version of sed on the CEREMADE cluster.
If the output file came from TraitLab, which uses a different naming system for outputs, then we need to edit the tree names before running treeloganalyser.
sed 's/\(tree\)\([0-9]\)/\1_\2/' tl-output.nex > tl-output.trees  SPR distances between output trees First download Chris Whidden’s rspr and run make.
Then edit rspr/R/rspR.r to point to the location of the rspr binary; in my case I replaced instances of command = "rspr" with command = "/home/users/kelly/rspr/rspr".
I then compute SPR distances between trees in R with
source("/home/users/kelly/rspr/R/rspR.r") t <- ape::read.nexus("output.trees") d <- rspr.matrix(t, "full", Inf)
summary: Install and run BEAST 2 From your home directory on the cluster, download a tarball of BEAST 2 (v2.6.3 was the latest at the time of writing) and extract the files.
title: Phylogenetic analyses on the CEREMADE server
